question.text: When should Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) be applied instead of standard CDD?

answer.text: When the customer is assessed as high risk, such as Politically Exposed Persons or clients from high-risk jurisdictions
When the customer requests a premium account with higher transaction limits
When the customer has been a long-standing client with no suspicious activity
When the customer opens their first account with the financial institution

explanation.text: **The Logic:** EDD is required for high-risk categories including PEPs, clients from high-risk countries, non-face-to-face clients, clients with dubious reputation, and HNIs. EDD involves deeper verification, senior management approval, and more frequent monitoring. **The Trap:** Long-standing clients with clean records might actually qualify for simplified due diligence, not EDD. First-time account opening requires standard CDD. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A and Series XXII (Compliance) test EDD triggers and requirements. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always get senior management approval before onboarding PEPs — document the source of funds and source of wealth thoroughly.
